mnemonics.title: DISTRIBUTED
mnemonics.content:
D - Diverse components
I - Independent computers
S - Single coherent system
T - Transparent to users
R - Resource sharing
I - Interconnected via network
B - Balanced scalability
U - Unified appearance
T - Through message passing
E - Excellent reliability
D - Distributed control

acronyms.usage: A fundamental theorem stating that distributed systems can only guarantee two of these three properties at any time during network partitions.
The software layer in distributed systems that provides common services and communication facilities between applications, hiding heterogeneity.
A reference model for network communication in distributed systems, with 7 layers defining how data is transmitted between applications.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a star topology where multiple computers (spokes) connect to a central network (hub), but each spoke appears as part of one unified system to end users who only see the center.

Picture an iceberg with 6 levels underwater (the actual distributed components) and only the top showing (the unified interface users see). Each underwater level represents a different transparency type.
Think of nodes as islands connected by bridges (network). Messages are boats carrying information between islands. The islands work independently but cooperate through these messages.

analogies.analogy: A distributed system is like a restaurant where you order food without knowing which kitchen staff member prepares it - you see one unified service. A computer network is like separate restaurants where you choose which one to visit - each maintains its identity.
Transparency in distributed systems is like using an ATM - you access your bank account without knowing which server processes your request or where your data is physically stored.
Message passing in distributed systems is like sending letters (messages) through postal service (network) - the sender and receiver don't share physical space but communicate through intermediaries.
